The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the abominations forbidden — "thou shalt not do so unto the LORD thy God: for every abomination to the LORD, which he hateth, have they done unto their gods; for even their sons and their daughters they have burnt in the fire to their gods": God is not to be worshipped after the manner of the nations, whose worship was full of abominations, even child sacrifice; the witnesses note the significance — the worship of God must be wholly free of the corruptions of heathen worship (the nations' worship was abominable to God, full of cruelty and filth, even the burning of their children; God's worship is not to borrow anything from it); the lesson — that God is to be worshipped as he appoints, never after the abominations he hates child sacrifice the height of its cruelty, "they sacrificed their sons and their daughters unto devils," Psalm 106:37;); the truth that man's religious inventions, far from honouring God, are abominations to him, "in vain they do worship me," Matthew 15:9; the figure — the purity and holiness of God's worship against the corruption of will-worship, the regulative principle that nothing of the heathen be brought into God's service; the warning that the will-worship of the nations is not zeal but abomination. The figure of the purity of God's worship against the abominations of the heathen.

On "thou shalt not do so unto the LORD thy God," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the prohibition. Keil and Ellicott note that God is not to be worshipped after the nations' manner, the purity of his worship guarded (the regulative truth, Matthew 15:9) — the prohibition (not so).

On "for every abomination to the LORD, which he hateth, have they done unto their gods; for even their sons and their daughters they have burnt in the fire to their gods," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the abominations. Wesley and Calvin note the nations' worship full of abominations God hates, even child sacrifice, nothing of it to be brought into God's service (the cruelty of idolatry, Psalm 106:37) — the figure (the purity of God's worship against the heathen corruption); the providence (the prohibition, the charge to add nothing and diminish nothing to follow, vv.32). The verse displays the prohibition of worshipping God after the abominations of the nations.

The witnesses agree the substance: God not worshipped after the nations (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the purity of worship: not after the abominations — the burning of their children the height of idolatry's cruelty (Psalm 106:37). The verse displays the worship-purity crux. The prohibition of worshipping God after the abominations of the nations displays the crux: thou shalt not do so unto the LORD thy God: for every abomination to the LORD, which he hateth, have they done unto their gods (Psalm 106:37; Matthew 15:9) — the worship of God to be wholly free of the corruptions and cruelties of the heathen, even their child sacrifice, the purity of his worship against the abominations he hates. The verse displays the charge to add nothing and diminish nothing (vv.32).
32:
"What thing soever I command you, observe to do it: thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it." The very brief verse notes the charge to keep God's commands whole, adding nothing and diminishing nothing. The grammatical voices note the charge. Calvin and Gill note that "what thing soever I command you, observe to do it: thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it" — they were to keep God's commands exactly, neither adding nor taking away; the what thing soever I command you, observe to do it (the command to keep all); the thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it (the charge to add nothing and diminish nothing); the figure (the charge to keep God's word whole and entire, the rule complete; the truth that man may not alter the worship and law of God, neither adding his own inventions nor cutting off God's commands; the figure of the sufficiency and inviolability of God's word, "ye shall not add unto the word... neither shall ye diminish ought from it," Deuteronomy 4:2; the regulative principle restated, the worship of God to follow his command exactly, "add thou not unto his words," Proverbs 30:6; the warning that closes the chapter, the rule of worship complete and inviolable, against the will-worship of the nations, vv.31). The verse closes the chapter, the charge to keep God's word whole.

On "What thing soever I command you, observe to do it," the witnesses note the command. Gill notes the command to keep all God's word (the obedience, Deuteronomy 4:2) — the command (observe to do it).

On "thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the inviolable word: "thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it" — the word to be kept whole, neither added to nor diminished (the sufficiency of God's word, Proverbs 30:6); the figure (the inviolability of God's word); the providence (the charge, the test of the false prophet to follow, ch.13). The verse displays the charge to keep God's commands whole, adding nothing and diminishing nothing.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "what thing soever I command you, observe to do it: thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it," the charge (Calvin, Gill); "What thing soever I command you, observe to do it," the command (Deuteronomy 4:2, Gill); "thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it," the inviolable word (Proverbs 30:6, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the command: to be kept. The verse displays the word: not added to. The verse displays the word: not diminished. The verse displays the rule: complete. The charge displays the sufficiency and inviolability of God's word. The verse displays the word: kept whole. The verse displays the addition: forbidden. The verse displays the subtraction: forbidden. The charge to keep God's commands whole, adding nothing and diminishing nothing, displays the inviolable word: what thing soever I command you, observe to do it: thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it (Deuteronomy 4:2; Proverbs 30:6) — God's commands to be kept exactly, neither added to nor taken from, the rule of worship complete and inviolable against the will-worship of the nations. The verse closes the chapter, before the test of the false prophet (ch.13).
